result.question:
Research for Robin, a compression-as-a-service API (Bun/TypeScript workers, ffmpeg with libopus available, CPU-only, no GPU; text and audio pipelines already implemented per prior verified research — Opus mono VBR for voice, TOON for JSON, those are ESTABLISHED, do not re-verify them). Four tracks, each researched by separate agents:

Track 1 — IMAGE COMPRESSION (the new pipeline, main track): best techniques and tools in 2026 for an API that compresses user-uploaded images (photos, screenshots, product images). (a) Format choice and real savings: WebP vs AVIF vs JPEG XL vs optimized JPEG/PNG — encode speed vs compression ratio vs browser/tool compatibility, recommended quality settings; (b) implementation on our stack: sharp/libvips vs ffmpeg vs imagemagick vs squoosh/codecs in Node/Bun — CPU cost, memory, licensing, maturity; (c) what Tinify/TinyPNG, Cloudinary, imgix, Kraken.io actually do (techniques, defaults, pricing per image/GB) and which operations users actually pay for (resize, quality-target compression, format conversion, metadata stripping); (d) perceptual quality metrics worth automating (SSIM, butteraugli, SSIMULACRA2) vs fixed quality settings.

Track 2 — TEXT, implementation feasibility only: running LLMLingua-2 (mBERT-110M or XLM-RoBERTa-355M) for prompt compression on CPU-only servers — ONNX Runtime in Node/Bun (does onnxruntime-node work under Bun?), transformers.js as alternative, realistic latency per 1K tokens on CPU, model licensing (MIT?) for commercial APIs, and any lighter-weight alternatives that ship as npm packages with no Python sidecar. If a Python microservice is unavoidable, what is the minimal deployment shape?

Track 3 — AUDIO, gaps only: beyond silence-trim + loudnorm + Opus encoding (already shipped), which audio operations do paying users of Auphonic/Cleanvoice/Descript actually value most (noise reduction/RNNoise/DeepFilterNet on CPU, two-pass loudnorm, filler-word removal), and which are feasible with plain ffmpeg filters vs need ML models?

Track 4 — INTEGRATION/DX: what makes compression APIs easy to adopt — study Tinify's API design (famously simple), Cloudinary URL-based transforms, and upload-handling patterns; SDK expectations (which languages first), webhook vs sync-response conventions for file APIs, and free-tier conventions that drive adoption.

Deliverable: per track, ranked recommendations by impact vs implementation effort ON OUR STACK, with concrete parameters (formats, quality settings, library choices, package names), explicitly marking what an MVP image pipeline should ship first and what to skip. Cite evidence; flag anything unverifiable.
result.summary: For Robin's new image pipeline, the verified evidence converges on one MVP shape: use sharp (libvips) directly in the existing Bun/TypeScript worker — sharp officially supports Bun (Node-API v9, documented `bun add sharp`), ships prebuilt binaries with bundled libvips (no compiler/Python/CLI sidecar), and is 4-5x faster than the quickest ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick settings on resize-inclusive pipelines. Ship WebP + AVIF output plus optimized JPEG/PNG: WebP and AVIF are the only formats rendered by default in all major browsers (JPEG XL is still flag-gated outside Safari as of mid-2026, and stock/prebuilt sharp cannot encode it anyway), while AVIF buys the largest savings (~56% vs JPEG in Cloudinary's published example; -51.6% BD-rate vs JPEG in the PCS 2025 benchmark) at a steep, tunable CPU cost — the benchmark's 3100x encode blowup is a slowest-preset libaom config, not what sharp at moderate speed costs, so effort/speed must be explicitly pinned and AVIF kept opt-in/async until measured. Do NOT rely on Bun's built-in Bun.Image: it cannot encode AVIF on Linux (AVIF/HEIC/TIFF depend on macOS/Windows OS backends), which is exactly the production target. On API design, copy Tinify: a single synchronous POST /shrink with HTTP Basic auth (`api:KEY`), 201 + Location result URL, dual ingestion (raw binary or source URL), no webhooks/polling for the core call — and monetize compression + resize/smart-crop + format conversion as fungible credits (Tinify bills each as one "compression"; metadata preservation is free), with direct-to-S3/GCS delivery and a ~500/month free tier as the adoption driver. Critically, Tracks 2 (text/LLMLingua-2) and 3 (audio gaps) produced ZERO surviving claims — they are unanswered, not negative.

findings.claim: MVP image library: sharp (libvips) in-process in the Bun worker. It officially supports Bun (Node-API v9), ships prebuilt binaries with bundled libvips (macOS x64/ARM64; Linux x64/ARM/ARM64/RISC-V/ppc64/s390x incl. musl; Windows; WASM opt-in), needs no system libvips, compiler toolchain, Python or CLI sidecar, and resizes 4-5x faster than the fastest ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick settings.
findings.evidence: Primary docs: "It can be used with all JavaScript runtimes that provide support for Node-API v9, including Node.js (>= 20.9.0), Deno and Bun"; the install page lists `bun add sharp` as a first-class path and has no Bun entry under Known conflicts; lovell/sharp#3511 ("Support for bun runtime") is closed against milestone v0.33.0 — deliberate support, not incidental. A verifier empirically ran `bun add sharp` (sharp@0.35.3) and completed a real WebP encode under Bun 1.2.23 with prebuilt libvips 8.18.3, no Node process. Perf: README says "Resizing an image is typically 4x-5x faster than using the quickest ImageMagick and GraphicsMagick settings due to its use of libvips", reproducible from sharp's published benchmark (c8a.xlarge, 2725x2225 JPEG -> lanczos3 720x588 -> q80: sharp 89.63 ops/s vs gm 20.92 vs imagemagick 16.23 = 4.3x / 5.5x), corroborated by libvips' own wiki (~7.8x vs ImageMagick convert). DEPLOYMENT CAVEATS from verifiers: run with `bun run`, NOT `bun build --compile` (bun#25395 / sharp#4283 fail to embed the platform .node); prefer a glibc debian-slim base image over Alpine or explicitly install @img/sharp-linuxmusl-* (sharp#4215/#4317); ensure optionalDependencies resolve so a macOS-generated lockfile still fetches @img/sharp-linux-{x64,arm64} in CI/Docker; pin Bun (1.3.4 has an arm64 compile-path regression). LICENSING: sharp Apache-2.0, libvips LGPL-3.0 — dynamic, unmodified use in a hosted SaaS creates no source-release obligation.
findings.vote: 3-0 (merged from claims [2],[5],[6],[7]; the 4x-5x perf claim [4] passed 2-1)

findings.claim: MVP output format set = WebP + AVIF + optimized JPEG/PNG. Skip JPEG XL. WebP and AVIF are the only formats rendered by default across all major browsers; JXL is still flag-gated outside Safari, AND stock/prebuilt sharp cannot encode it — a JXL path would need a separate encoder (libjxl/cjxl, ffmpeg, imagemagick) or a custom libvips+libjxl build.
findings.evidence: PCS 2025 (peer-reviewed): "WebP and AVIF are supported by all standard browsers, while JPEG XL and HEIC are only supported by Safari; however, Firefox is expected to support JPEG XL." Corroborated July 2026: caniuse/jpegxl (page dated 2026-03-05) = 13.6% global support, "no major browsers have enabled JPEG XL support by default" — Chrome 145+ and Firefox 152 ship decoders behind flags only; WebP ~97-98% and AVIF ~94-95% are default-on. Sharp's homepage: reads {JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, AVIF, TIFF, SVG}, writes {JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, AVIF, TIFF, raw}; install page lists prebuilt codec support as "JPEG, PNG, Ultra HDR, WebP, AVIF, TIFF, GIF and SVG (input)" — JXL absent from both. Not an argument from silence: lovell/sharp#2731 is titled "add experimental support for the JPEG-XL format, requires libvips compiled with support for libjxl, prebuilt binaries will not support this." Commercial validation: Tinify's supported set is AVIF/WebP/JPEG/PNG (+animated PNG input) with a wildcard that "returns the smallest of Tinify's supported image types, currently AVIF, WebP, JPEG and PNG" — no JXL. TIME-SENSITIVE: watch for Chrome flipping JXL default-on (rumored H2 2026); even then sharp would still need a separate encoder.
findings.vote: 3-0 (merged from [1],[3],[8],[14]; two weaker restatements were refuted for overreach, not for the format lists)

findings.claim: AVIF is the biggest savings lever (~50-56% vs JPEG) but is the dominant CPU cost — encode effort/speed must be explicitly pinned, and AVIF should ship as an opt-in / async-queued tier while WebP is the default synchronous path.
findings.evidence: PCS 2025 benchmark (10 codecs, 310 configs, 101 QPs, 28 IQA metrics): "AVIF (AOM) implementation achieves the highest coding efficiency" and "Compared to JPEG 420 baseline coding, encoding time differed by up to 3100 times (5.6ms / 17,500ms), with a BD-rate (SSIMULACRA2) improvement of -51.6%." Cloudinary's published same-source comparison: AVIF 14.6 KB < WebP 16.1 KB < JPEG XL 21.4 KB < JPEG 33.5 KB < PNG 190.0 KB, i.e. AVIF ~56% smaller than JPEG, WebP ~52%. QUALIFIERS: (a) the 17.5s figure is the slowest-preset libaom config on ONE 2048x1216 image — do not budget from it, but do treat AVIF encode as O(seconds) not O(ms); AVIF *decode* is fast (~4.5x JPEG). (b) The same paper notes "Considering computation time, JPEG2000 also offers a good balance" — the efficiency winner is not the throughput winner. (c) Cloudinary's numbers are one vendor-chosen image at unstated (likely q_auto) settings: do NOT quote ~56% as expected savings on arbitrary uploads, and do NOT read their JXL=21.4KB as evidence JXL is worse than WebP generally (that reflects their encoder tuning; independent studies put JXL near AVIF for photos). RECOMMENDATION: default sync path = WebP q80 (universal, ~52%); AVIF q~50-60 as an opt-in flag or queued job with an explicit effort/speed cap (sharp `effort` 4-6, not the max).

findings.claim: Do NOT replace sharp with Bun's built-in Bun.Image for the compression pipeline. It exists, is zero-install, and covers resize/rotate/flip/modulate + JPEG/PNG/WebP with sharp-compatible filters — but on Linux (the production target) it cannot encode AVIF/HEIC/TIFF at all, and has no crop/composite/sharpen/ICC handling.
findings.evidence: Bun's release post: Bun.Image handles "JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, and BMP — plus HEIC, AVIF, and TIFF on macOS and Windows", described as "a drop-in alternative to sharp for common server-side image operations", with a chainable pipeline: .resize(w,h,{filter,fit,withoutEnlargement}), .rotate(90|180|270), .flip()/.flop(), .modulate({brightness,saturation}), encoders .jpeg({quality,progressive}) / .png({compressionLevel,palette,colors,dither}) / .webp({quality,lossless}) / .avif({quality}), terminals .bytes()/.blob()/.write()/.metadata()/.placeholder() (thumbhash). Filters are exactly sharp's kernel set (nearest, box, bilinear, cubic, mitchell, lanczos2, lanczos3, mks2013, mks2021; lanczos3 default). BUT the runtime docs state JPEG/PNG/WebP/GIF/BMP use statically linked codecs while HEIC/AVIF/TIFF depend on OS backends (ImageIO+vImage on macOS, WIC on Windows) — on Linux those throw error.code === "ERR_IMAGE_FORMAT_UNSUPPORTED". AVIF *encode* on macOS is further limited to Apple Silicon M3+. Bun's own benchmarks (70x metadata, 1.2-1.38x resize) are vendor-selected and were contradicted by a third-party run (~8% delta vs sharp) — ignore them. VERDICT: Bun.Image is a fine fast path for thumbnails/thumbhash placeholders (genuinely nice, dependency-free), but sharp remains required for AVIF, ICC/color management, smart-crop and composite. PHRASING NOTE: "no AVIF on Linux" is true only OF Bun.Image — sharp/libvips (libaom/libheif), ffmpeg (libaom-av1/libsvtav1) and avifenc all encode AVIF fine on Linux.
findings.vote: 3-0 (merged from [9],[10],[11]; [10] passed 2-1 only due to its over-broad 'AVIF not available on Linux' phrasing)

findings.claim: API design (Track 4): copy Tinify's shape. Single synchronous call — POST raw image bytes (or JSON {"source":{"url":"..."}}) to /shrink with HTTP Basic auth (literal username `api`, password = API key, HTTPS only) -> 201 Created + Location: /output/{id}. No SDK, no key exchange, no webhook, no job polling for the core operation. Post-processing (resize, convert, preserve metadata, store to S3/GCS) is done by POSTing JSON to the returned output URL.
findings.evidence: Canonical call: `curl https://api.tinify.com/shrink --user api:YOUR_API_KEY --data-binary @unoptimized.jpg`. Reference docs: "Authentication to the API is done with HTTP Basic Auth ... All requests must be made over an encrypted HTTPS connection" (Authorization = Base64 of `api:KEY`); "Compression will start as soon as you upload a file or provide the URL to the image"; response is 201 + Location (e.g. https://api.tinify.com/output/2xnsp7jn34e5) plus a Compression-Count header returned immediately. Dual ingestion: "You can either upload images directly or specify a URL to the image you want to compress." Delivery: "You can instruct the API to save the optimized images directly in your Amazon S3 or Google Cloud Storage buckets. When doing this, you may also set custom Cache-Control and Expires headers." SDKs exist ("We also maintain client libraries in various languages") but are explicitly optional — plain curl performs the full core operation, which is a large part of why the API is considered easy to adopt. PRECISION NITS: it is not literally one endpoint (/shrink -> GET|POST /output/{id}); and `Expires` is attested only on the how-it-works page, not in the /reference store docs — verify the header name before implementing. IMPLICATION FOR ROBIN: keep BullMQ for AVIF/large files, but the default image call should return a result URL synchronously; webhooks are an escape hatch, not the primary contract.
findings.vote: 3-0 (merged from [13],[16],[17]; two variants were refuted for over-claiming 'user never picks quality' and for listing Azure)

findings.claim: Monetization (Track 1c / Track 4): bill compression, resize/crop, and format conversion as fungible credits — all three are billable, metadata preservation is free. Smart-crop (free-standing object detection for product photos) and quality-preserving resize (gamma-correct scaling, bicubic transparency edge correction, natural sharpening) are the differentiated paid value-adds beyond raw re-encode. Free tier ~500 ops/month.
findings.evidence: Tinify reference, verbatim: "Resizing counts as one additional compression. For example, if you upload a single image and retrieve the optimized version plus 2 resized versions this will count as 3 compressions in total." / "Image converting will count as one additional compression." / "Preserving metadata will not count as an extra compression. However, in the background the image will be created again with the additional metadata." Saving to S3/GCS also counts as an additional compression. Pricing page: "Tinify only charges when images are compressed", "500 free compressions each month." Feature depth (how-it-works): "Perfect image resizing" with "correct gamma scaling, bicubic transparency edge correction and natural image sharpening"; smart cropping "detects the areas of interest and will keep them centered", is "designed for images with free-standing objects on a plain background, such as product photos", and "can add background space where needed or crop away less important areas." (Tinify says "smart algorithm"/object detection — never "AI".) DESIGN TAKEAWAY: one fungible credit unit, not per-operation SKUs; each output artifact = one credit. Also cheap and high-perceived-value: Cloudinary-style f_auto / Tinify-style `*/*` wildcard that returns the smallest supported type — trivial once you already encode WebP+AVIF.

findings.claim: SKIP perceptual-metric-driven quality targeting (SSIMULACRA2 / butteraugli auto-tuning) in the MVP. Ship fixed quality presets, and use SSIMULACRA2 OFFLINE to calibrate them once against a representative corpus.
findings.evidence: The claim "SSIMULACRA2 and Butteraugli are the metrics of record ... supporting automated perceptual-metric-driven quality targeting over fixed quality settings" was REFUTED 1-2 by the verifiers — the inference (encoders expose tune options -> therefore automate per-image metric targeting) was judged an overreach on the source. What IS established: SSIMULACRA2 is the BD-rate metric used in the PCS 2025 benchmark (28 IQA metrics evaluated), so it is a legitimate OFFLINE evaluation tool. Reasoning for the negative recommendation: per-image binary-search-to-metric-target multiplies encode cost roughly 3-5x on the most expensive codec (AVIF), which is precisely the wrong trade on a CPU-only worker. Start with fixed presets (JPEG/WebP q80; AVIF q50-60, effort 4-6) and calibrate once. A content-type heuristic (photo vs screenshot/UI-text vs product-on-white) is likely worth more than metric tuning, since flat-color/text content rings badly under photographic AVIF/WebP presets and sometimes loses to lossless-WebP/PNG outright — but that segmentation is itself unresearched.
findings.vote: refuted 1-2 (reported here as a negative/skip recommendation)

findings.claim: Tracks 2 (text / LLMLingua-2 on CPU) and 3 (audio gaps beyond silence-trim + loudnorm + Opus) produced ZERO surviving verified claims in this round. Nothing here supports or refutes onnxruntime-node under Bun, transformers.js latency, LLMLingua-2 licensing, RNNoise/DeepFilterNet on CPU, two-pass loudnorm, or filler-word removal.
sources: (absence of evidence — no Track 2 or Track 3 claim reached the verification stage)
findings.evidence: All 19 surviving claims and all 6 refuted claims come from Track 1 (image formats/libraries) and Track 4 (Tinify/Cloudinary API design and pricing). The synthesis input contains no claim touching ONNX Runtime, transformers.js, LLMLingua-2, mBERT/XLM-RoBERTa, Python sidecar deployment shapes, RNNoise, DeepFilterNet, Auphonic/Cleanvoice/Descript feature demand, or ffmpeg audio filters. These two tracks must be re-run before any decision is made on them; treating this report as a verdict on them would be a category error.

result.caveats:
1) TRACK COVERAGE IS INCOMPLETE. Only Tracks 1 and 4 produced verified claims. Track 2 (LLMLingua-2 / ONNX Runtime under Bun / transformers.js / Python sidecar shape) and Track 3 (RNNoise/DeepFilterNet, two-pass loudnorm, filler-word removal) have ZERO evidence here. Absence of evidence is not a negative finding — re-run them.
2) NO PRICING DATA BEYOND TINIFY. The brief asked for Cloudinary, imgix and Kraken.io pricing per image/GB; only Tinify's model (500 free/month; one fungible "compression" credit per output artifact) and Cloudinary's f_auto behavior were verified. Competitive pricing positioning is unresolved.
3) VENDOR-SOURCED NUMBERS. Cloudinary's AVIF/WebP/JXL byte comparison is ONE vendor-chosen image at unstated quality — do not quote ~56%/~52% as expected savings on real uploads. Sharp's 4-5x is a vendor self-benchmark (methodology published and reproducible) that specifically describes RESIZE-INCLUSIVE pipelines; for pure re-compression at native dimensions the wall-clock is codec-dominated and the 4-5x edge should NOT be assumed. Bun's Bun.Image benchmarks (70x metadata, 1.2-1.38x resize) are self-selected and were contradicted by a third-party run (~8% delta) — ignore them.
4) THE 3100x AVIF ENCODE FIGURE IS A WORST CASE — slowest libaom preset, single 2048x1216 image, per-QP averages. It is not what sharp at effort 4-6 costs. We have NO measured AVIF encode cost on our actual target hardware; that must be measured before pricing or SLAs.
5) TIME-SENSITIVE (H2 2026): JPEG XL is flag-gated in Chrome 145+ and Firefox 152; Chrome default-on is rumored for H2 2026. If it lands, revisit the WebP+AVIF-only recommendation — but note stock sharp still cannot encode JXL, so it needs a separate encoder regardless.
6) DEPLOYMENT GOTCHAS (verified, easy to trip on): sharp under Bun breaks with `bun build --compile` (platform .node not embedded) — use `bun run`. Alpine/musl + Bun is the most common sharp install failure — prefer glibc/debian-slim, and ensure optionalDependencies resolve so a macOS-generated lockfile still fetches @img/sharp-linux-{x64,arm64} in CI/Docker. Bun 1.3.4 has an arm64 compile-path regression.
7) Tinify's `Expires` header for S3/GCS store is attested only on the marketing page, not the API reference — verify before implementing.
8) One normative statement ("a modern compression API is expected to ship AVIF+WebP conversion") is competitive framing inferred from a single vendor, not a verified fact — treat as a design recommendation.
openQuestions: What is the actual AVIF encode wall-clock and peak RSS on our production worker (CPU-only, N vCPU) via sharp, across effort 4/6/8 and quality 45/55/65, for representative 12MP photos, 4K screenshots and 1500px product images? Pricing, queue sizing, and whether AVIF can ever be synchronous all hang on this number, and no verified source answers it.

Tracks 2 and 3 were never answered: does onnxruntime-node actually load under Bun (Node-API compat), what is realistic LLMLingua-2 latency per 1K tokens on CPU, and is the model license MIT-compatible for a commercial API? Separately, which of RNNoise / two-pass loudnorm / filler-word removal is achievable with plain ffmpeg filters vs requires an ML sidecar?

What do Cloudinary, imgix and Kraken.io actually charge per image / per GB, and what does a competitive free tier look like beyond Tinify's 500/month? We currently have no basis for pricing positioning.

Should quality presets branch on content type (photo vs screenshot/UI-text vs product-on-white)? Flat-color and text content rings under photographic AVIF/WebP presets and can lose outright to lossless-WebP/PNG — this segmentation may matter more than perceptual-metric tuning and is entirely unresearched.

Does Tinify-style smart-crop (free-standing object detection for product photos) have a practical CPU-only implementation on libvips/sharp (e.g. sharp's attention/entropy crop strategies), or does it need an ML model? It is one of Tinify's clearest billable differentiators and we have zero evidence on its build cost.

refuted.claim: SSIMULACRA2 and Butteraugli are the color-native perceptual metrics of record for modern codec tuning, and encoders (AVIF AOM, Jpegli, JPEG XL) expose explicit tune options for them - supporting automated perceptual-metric-driven quality targeting over fixed quality settings.
sharp resizes images typically 4x-5x faster than the fastest ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick settings, because it uses libvips with streaming region-based processing rather than spawning child processes.

sharp encodes JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF and AVIF, but JPEG XL is not listed among its output formats — so an MVP image pipeline on sharp can ship WebP + AVIF + optimized JPEG/PNG, while JPEG XL would require a different encoder.

Tinify's API supports AVIF, WebP, JPEG and PNG as its compression/conversion formats — notably no JPEG XL — indicating AVIF+WebP+optimized JPEG/PNG is the commercially validated format set for a compression API in 2026.

Compression is synchronous and format-agnostic to the caller: Tinify auto-detects the input type and routes to the TinyPNG or TinyJPG engine, supporting AVIF, WebP, JPEG and PNG — the user never picks a quality setting or codec.

Post-compression operations are applied by POSTing JSON to the result URL (/output/{id}): resize (scale, fit, cover, thumb), format conversion (AVIF/WebP/JPEG/PNG, returning the smallest of the requested types), metadata preservation (copyright, creation date, GPS — JPEG only), and direct upload to S3/GCS/Azure. These are the billable value-adds beyond raw compression.
